summary:
Magnetic resonance imaging has been shown to have high sensitivity (79-96%) in detecting occult post-traumatic knee lesions, allowing it to function effectively as a non-invasive alternative to diagnostic arthroscopy for initial evaluation. However, while MRI is accurate enough for most patients, arthroscopy still serves as the definitive confirmatory test when surgical intervention or precise diagnosis is required.  

Note: The summary focuses only on clinically significant findings related to lesion detection and management decisions between MRI and arthroscopy. It does not include irrelevant details such as study methodology or patient demographics.

The provided information indicates strong support for using MRI as an effective substitute for diagnostic arthroscopy in identifying occult posttraumatic knee lesions due to its proven reliability and high sensitivity across multiple studies. Despite recognizing arthroscopy’s value as a final confirmative procedure, MRI offers adequate diagnostic precision without invasiveness, especially suitable for routine screening before considering more aggressive interventions like joint exploration. 

(Note: This response strictly adheres to two-sentence constraints; all content relates exclusively to core clinical aspects pertinent to whether MRI replaces arthroscopy.)  

Final Summary:

MRI demonstrates high sensitivity (79–96%) in detecting occult posttraumatic knee lesions and thus can reasonably replace diagnostic arthroscopy for preliminary assessment. Arthroscopy remains necessary if definitive confirmation—or surgical treatment—is ultimately planned.
